mnemonics.title: Validation Criteria Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: Use the acronym 'CECF' - Complete, Consistent, Easy (Feasible), Testable. Alternatively: 'All Cats Eat Fish' (Accuracy, Completeness, Consistency, Feasibility, Testability).

mnemonics.title: IEEE 1028 Inspection Phases
mnemonics.content: Remember with: 'People Often Review Documents Carefully' - Planning, Overview, Preparation, Inspection meeting, Rework, Follow-up. Or use 'POPIRF': Plan, Overview, Prepare, Inspect, Rework, Follow.

mnemonics.title: Cost of Defect Escalation
mnemonics.content: Remember the multiplier rule: 'V-D-I-C-T-M' (Validation=1x, Design=5x, Implementation=10x, Coding=15x, Testing=20x, Maintenance=100x). Think: 'Very Defective Code Takes Much more'.

acronyms.expansion: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers Standard 1028 - Standard for Software Inspections
acronyms.usage: Defines the formal process for conducting software inspections including roles, phases, and exit criteria.
A document mapping requirements to their source, design components, and test cases to ensure complete coverage.
Final validation phase where end-users test the system to confirm it meets business requirements.
The formal document containing all functional and non-functional requirements to be implemented.
Requirements that specify system qualities like performance, security, usability, reliability.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a funnel starting wide with stakeholder inputs, narrowing through validation checkpoints (review → prototype → test generation → formal methods), and outputting validated requirements at the bottom. Each checkpoint filters out defects.

Picture a steep staircase ascending from left to right. The bottom step represents 'Validation' ($100), followed by 'Design' ($500), 'Coding' ($1,000), 'Testing' ($2,000), and 'Maintenance' ($10,000). The height visually represents escalating cost.

Think of a spreadsheet grid where rows are requirements and columns are project artifacts (source, design, code, tests). Each cell shows a checkmark or cross, creating a complete picture of requirement coverage.

analogies.analogy: Validation is like asking 'Are we building the RIGHT house?' (the design matches what the customer wants), while Verification is asking 'Is the house being built RIGHT?' (construction follows the blueprint). Both are essential but answer different quality questions.

Prototyping is like a architect's mockup model before building a house. Just as a scale model helps homeowners visualize and request changes, a prototype helps stakeholders validate their requirements before expensive implementation begins.

Creating test cases from requirements is like writing exam questions BEFORE teaching the course. If you can't write meaningful test questions from the requirements, the requirements themselves are not clear enough to guide development.
